project.title: LLM Foundations & Practice
project.goal: Understand how Transformer architectures enable language understanding and generation, then apply them in real tasks

modules.title: Tokens & Vocabulary
modules.subtitle: From text to discrete symbols
modules.quote: The world of language models starts with the tokenizer
modules.keyInsight: BPE balances frequency and granularity—keep common words intact, split rare words into subword pieces
logicChain: Raw text cannot be fed directly into the model
We must map text into discrete tokens
BPE starts from characters and merges frequent pairs
Vocabulary size controls granularity and sequence length
Each token is mapped to a unique integer ID
counterexamples: Pure character tokenization keeps all info but makes sequences too long; attention cost grows O(n²)

modules.title: Embedding Space
modules.subtitle: From symbols to semantic vectors
modules.quote: Similar meanings end up close in vector space
modules.keyInsight: Token IDs are mapped into a dense embedding space; positional encoding injects order information
logicChain: Token IDs are discrete and lack semantics
Embedding matrices map IDs into dense vectors
Training shapes vectors so semantic similarity is spatial closeness
Transformer layers are permutation-invariant; order must be injected
Positional encoding is added to token embeddings
examples: king - man + woman ≈ queen shows linear structure in embedding space
counterexamples: One-hot encoding is sparse and cannot express semantic similarity

modules.title: Attention Mechanism
modules.subtitle: The art of weighted aggregation
modules.quote: Attention is similarity-driven weighting of Value vectors
modules.keyInsight: Multi-head attention lets the model capture different relationship patterns in parallel
logicChain: Each token produces Q, K, V
Q·K^T / √d_k yields attention scores
Softmax normalizes scores into probabilities
Probabilities weight V to produce outputs
Multiple heads run in parallel subspaces
Concat all heads and project to model dimension
examples: 'The cat sat on the mat because it was tired' — 'it' attends most to 'cat'
counterexamples: RNN hidden states compress the whole sequence; attention removes this bottleneck

pitfalls.point: Cannot clearly explain the output projection after multi-head concat

modules.title: Transformer Structure
modules.subtitle: Encoder–decoder symphony
modules.quote: Residual connections and layer norm keep deep networks alive
logicChain: Self-attention captures token interactions
Residuals preserve signal and ease optimization
Layer norm stabilizes distributions
FFN applies position-wise nonlinearity
(learning in progress...)

pitfalls.point: Confuses information flow between self-attention and cross-attention

modules.title: Pretraining Objectives
modules.subtitle: Self-supervision design
modules.quote: Predicting the next token is simple, yet powerful

modules.title: Loss & Optimization
modules.subtitle: How gradients shape language ability
modules.quote: Cross-entropy measures divergence between predicted and true distributions

modules.title: Fine-tuning & Alignment
modules.subtitle: From general to controllable
modules.quote: RLHF teaches the model to navigate human preferences

modules.title: Prompt Engineering
modules.subtitle: Programming with language
modules.quote: Great prompts build scaffolding for the model's reasoning

modules.title: Scaling Laws
modules.subtitle: Power and limits of scale
modules.quote: Parameters, data, compute—three variables follow elegant power laws

modules.title: Emergence & Reasoning
modules.subtitle: When scale sparks new abilities
modules.quote: At sufficient scale, models exhibit capabilities not explicitly trained

modules.title: Context Window
modules.subtitle: Length vs memory trade-offs
modules.quote: Larger windows mean longer reasoning but higher cost

modules.title: Systematic Review
modules.subtitle: Make the model your own knowledge
modules.quote: The final lesson is to organize knowledge in your own words
